h1: Part 50 vs Part 52 vs Part 53: the three NRC reactor licensing pathways, explained
meta_title: Part 50 vs Part 52 vs Part 53: NRC pathways | Invariant
meta_description: A side-by-side comparison of 10 CFR Part 50, Part 52, and Part 53 — history, structure, timelines, and which advanced reactor developers use each in 2026.
canonical_summary:
10 CFR Part 50, Part 52, and Part 53 are the three frameworks the U.S. Nuclear Regulatory Commission uses to license commercial power reactors. Part 50, first published in 1956, is the original two-step pathway: a Construction Permit (CP) based on a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report, followed years later by a separate Operating License based on a Final Safety Analysis Report. Part 52, adopted in 1989, is the one-step alternative: a Combined License (COL) that authorizes both construction and conditional operation, typically referencing an Early Site Permit and a Standard Design Certification so that site and design questions are resolved up front and verified after construction through Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC). Part 53, finalized on March 30, 2026 and effective April 29, 2026, is an optional risk-informed, technology-inclusive framework mandated by the Nuclear Energy Innovation and Modernization Act (NEIMA) of 2019; it replaces the LWR-centric General Design Criteria of Part 50 Appendix A with performance-based safety criteria, Licensing-Basis Events derived from PRA or Systematic Risk Evaluation, and graded seismic and siting requirements.

The three pathways coexist rather than supersede each other. As of mid-2026, the operating U.S. fleet was licensed under Part 50; the only Part 52 plants in commercial operation are Vogtle Units 3 and 4 (AP1000); and Part 53 has no applicants yet but is expected to attract first movers in the 2026–2028 window. The choice among pathways turns on design maturity, fleet strategy, and tolerance for upfront regulatory burden: Part 50's CP-then-OL split suits first-of-a-kind non-LWR demonstrations where design will evolve during construction (Kairos Hermes, TerraPower Natrium, X-energy/Dow Long Mott, Holtec SMR-300 at Palisades); Part 52's design-once-build-many model suits mature standardized designs (NuScale, Westinghouse AP300); Part 53 suits novel coolants and risk-informed safety cases that do not fit either legacy framework (Newcleo LFR, future Oklo, future Kairos commercial).

sections.heading: The three pathways at a glance
paragraphs: 10 CFR Part 50, titled 'Domestic Licensing of Production and Utilization Facilities,' is the original NRC reactor regulation, in force since 1956. It implements a two-step process: a Construction Permit issued against a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report (PSAR) under § 50.34(a), followed years later by a separate Operating License issued against a Final Safety Analysis Report (FSAR) under § 50.34(b) and § 50.57. Design information at the CP stage is preliminary and can change during construction. Essentially every operating U.S. commercial reactor was licensed under Part 50, and after a long hiatus the pathway has been revived for non-LWR demonstrations.

10 CFR Part 52, 'Licenses, Certifications, and Approvals for Nuclear Power Plants,' was issued April 18, 1989 (54 FR 15372) as an alternative — not a replacement — to Part 50. It bundles three composable instruments: an Early Site Permit (Subpart A) that banks site approval for up to 20 years; a Standard Design Certification (Subpart B) that codifies a complete reactor design as an Appendix to Part 52; and a Combined License (Subpart C) that authorizes construction and conditional operation in a single proceeding, with Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC) verified before the § 52.103(g) finding that permits fuel load.

10 CFR Part 53, 'Risk-Informed, Technology-Inclusive Regulatory Framework for Advanced Reactors,' was published as a final rule on March 30, 2026 and became effective April 29, 2026, more than a year ahead of the NEIMA deadline of December 31, 2027. It is optional: applicants may still choose Part 50 or Part 52. Part 53 replaces the LWR-centric General Design Criteria of Part 50 Appendix A with high-level performance criteria; replaces the single Design-Basis Accident with a full Licensing-Basis Event spectrum (Anticipated Operational Occurrences, Design-Basis Events, Beyond-Design-Basis Events) derived from PRA or Systematic Risk Evaluation; and grades seismic, siting, staffing, and codes-and-standards requirements by safety significance.

sections.heading: History: 1956 to 2026
paragraphs: Part 50 was first published in the Federal Register on January 19, 1956 by the Atomic Energy Commission, the NRC's predecessor. A 1957 amendment to the Atomic Energy Act required mandatory hearings for both Construction Permits and Operating Licenses, which created the administrative bottleneck the rule has carried ever since. In 1962, Congress removed the mandatory hearing for uncontested OL proceedings and the AEC issued § 50.59, the change-control rule that lets licensees modify the facility without prior approval when defined screening criteria are met. Appendix A (General Design Criteria) and Appendix B (Quality Assurance) were promulgated in their modern form in 1971. Appendix R (fire protection), the direct regulatory response to the March 22, 1975 Browns Ferry cable-spreading-room fire, was published in November 1980 and became effective February 17, 1981; roughly 60 plants required plant-specific exemptions during backfit.

Part 52 was adopted on April 18, 1989 to address two failures of the Part 50 era: massive cost and schedule overruns caused by late design changes during construction, and the ability of intervenors to block plants at the OL stage after construction was complete (Shoreham being the canonical example). The Energy Policy Act of 1992 codified the combined-license concept at the statutory level. A substantial Part 52 update in 2007 increased regulatory certainty, aligned Parts 50 and 52, and clarified ITAAC procedures. The first Combined Licenses were issued February 10, 2012 for Vogtle Units 3 and 4. Vogtle Unit 3 received its § 52.103(g) finding on August 3, 2022; Unit 4's finding followed on July 28, 2023.

Part 53 began with NEIMA, signed January 14, 2019, which directed the NRC to complete a technology-inclusive rulemaking by December 31, 2027. The proposed rule was published October 31, 2024. The ADVANCE Act, signed July 9, 2024, added microreactor provisions and 100% fee reimbursement for the first advanced reactor licensed under Part 53. After 158 public comments, the Commission voted to approve the final rule on March 25–26, 2026; it was published March 30, 2026 and took effect April 29, 2026.

sections.heading: Key differences side by side
paragraphs: The three frameworks differ along several axes — process structure, design criteria, accident analysis, siting, and finality — and the table below captures the practical distinctions an applicant or reviewer would encounter.

subsections.heading: Process structure
paragraphs: Part 50 is two-step: CP (§ 50.35) then OL (§ 50.57), with a separate NRC review at each stage. Part 52 is one-step: a Combined License (§ 52.97) that authorizes construction and conditional operation in a single proceeding, with ITAAC verified before the § 52.103(g) finding. Part 53 offers a menu under Subpart H: applicants can pursue construction permits, operating licenses, combined licenses, standard design approvals, design certifications, or manufacturing licenses, using the risk-informed safety framework of Subparts B through G.

subsections.heading: Design criteria and accident analysis
paragraphs: Parts 50 and 52 anchor on the 64 General Design Criteria in Appendix A to Part 50, which embed LWR assumptions; non-LWR applicants must use Principal Design Criteria from Regulatory Guide 1.232. Accident analysis is deterministic, organized around a Design-Basis Accident with conservative assumptions. Part 53 replaces that structure with high-level performance criteria in §§ 53.210 and 53.220 and a Licensing-Basis Event spectrum in § 53.240 (AOO ≥ 10⁻² /plant-yr, DBE 10⁻² to 10⁻⁴, BDBE 10⁻⁴ to ~5×10⁻⁷), analyzed under § 53.450 against the LMP frequency-consequence curve.

subsections.heading: Seismic, siting, and codes
paragraphs: Parts 50 and 52 use a single Safe-Shutdown Earthquake bounding the whole plant (Appendix S) with a strong remote-site preference (Part 100, RG 4.7). Part 53 introduces Seismic Design Categories (§ 53.480) tied to ANSI/ANS-2.26 and ASCE/SEI 43, allowing safety-significance-graded ground motions, and § 53.530 permits alternative siting — including in densely populated centers above ~25,000 residents — if societal benefits outweigh risks. Codes and standards (§ 50.55a) are mandated in Parts 50/52; Part 53 allows applicant-chosen codes for non-safety SSCs and risk-informed treatment for safety SSCs.

sections.heading: Who uses which pathway in 2026
paragraphs: As of mid-2026, the operating U.S. fleet — 95 reactors, including Watts Bar Unit 2 (OL 2015–2016) — was licensed under Part 50. The pathway has been deliberately revived for first-of-a-kind non-LWR projects because the CP+OL split tolerates iterative design maturation during construction, and the PSAR/FSAR architecture fits demonstration reactors that have no equivalent in Part 52.

Part 52 is the home of the U.S. nuclear renaissance fleet. Vogtle 3 and 4 (Southern Nuclear, AP1000) are the only Part 52 plants in commercial operation. V.C. Summer 2 and 3 received COLs March 30, 2012 but were abandoned in August 2017 after Westinghouse's bankruptcy. South Texas Project 3 and 4 received COLs in 2016 but were never built. NuScale received the first SMR Design Certification (Appendix G to Part 52) in 2023 and a Standard Design Approval for the 77-MWe US460 on May 29, 2025. Westinghouse is pursuing an AP300 SDA with a Part 52 Design Certification targeted for 2027.

Part 53 has no formal applicants as of mid-2026 but is the expected pathway for novel-coolant designs. Newcleo's lead-cooled fast reactor began U.S. pre-application engagement March 24, 2026 with Part 53 as the likely framework. Kairos Power's commercial KP-FHR (after the Part 50 Hermes and Hermes 2 demonstrations), Oklo's next-generation Aurora, and several microreactor developers are widely expected to move under Part 53.

subsections.heading: Part 50 advanced-reactor docket
paragraphs: Kairos Power received the first non-LWR CP in decades for Hermes on December 12, 2023, followed by Hermes 2 (twin-unit) CPs on November 21, 2024. TerraPower's Natrium CP for Kemmerer Unit 1 was issued December 31, 2025 — the first commercial sodium-fast-reactor CP in over 40 years — with construction beginning April 2026. X-energy/Dow's Long Mott (four Xe-100 units at Seadrift, TX) was docketed May 12, 2025 with an 18-month review schedule and a CP target of Q1 2027. Holtec submitted a Part 50 CPA for two SMR-300 units at Palisades in late December 2025; the NRC docketed it February 27, 2026.

subsections.heading: Part 52 docket
paragraphs: Beyond Vogtle, NuScale's certified Power Module (Appendix G) and US460 SDA dominate the active Part 52 inventory. Oklo's restructured Aurora powerhouse is pursuing a phased Part 52 COL after the January 6, 2022 denial-without-prejudice of its original COLA; pre-application readiness was confirmed by NRC in July 2025. Westinghouse's AP300 is in pre-application with a 2027 DC target. Rolls-Royce SMR (a 470-MWe PWR) is in UK GDA Step 3 and has filed a U.S. pre-application white paper, with Part 52 the likely framework given the AP1000-style architecture.

sections.heading: How to pick a pathway
paragraphs: The choice among Parts 50, 52, and 53 turns on three questions: how mature is the design, how many plants will be built, and how much regulatory novelty does the safety case carry. The answers map onto pathway strengths the NRC built deliberately into each framework.

Choose Part 50 when the design is still maturing, the project is first-of-a-kind, and the schedule cannot wait for a fully finalized FSAR. The CP is issued against a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report that need only show 'reasonable assurance' the final design will protect public health and safety. Design changes during construction do not require re-opening a certified-design rulemaking. The trade-off is a separate Operating License review against the as-built facility, with no design-certification finality protection. This is why Kairos, TerraPower, X-energy/Dow, and Holtec all chose Part 50 for their first commercial units.

Choose Part 52 when the design is essentially complete, the developer wants to build the same plant at multiple sites, and the priority is closing off post-construction intervention risk. A Design Certification (15-year original duration, with NRC extending toward 40 years; AP1000 currently extended to February 27, 2026) gives the design the force of regulation under § 52.63, and a COL referencing a DC and an ESP precludes relitigation of those issues at the COL stage. The trade-off is essentially OL-level design detail at application time and a high upfront review burden.

Choose Part 53 when the safety case does not fit the LWR-centric Appendix A General Design Criteria, when the design supports a robust PRA or Systematic Risk Evaluation, or when the project benefits from graded seismic, siting, or staffing requirements. The first applicant under Part 53 receives 100% NRC fee reimbursement under the ADVANCE Act. Part 53's optional licensing menu (Subpart H) means an applicant can still pick a CP+OL or a COL structure within the risk-informed framework.

sections.heading: Timelines and documentation
paragraphs: NEIMA and Executive Order 14300 (2025) set an 18-month target for NRC decisions on new reactor applications, dramatically tighter than historical practice. Recent advanced-reactor experience shows the target is achievable: TerraPower's Kemmerer Unit 1 safety review finished in 18 months, nine months ahead of schedule, and the X-energy/Dow Long Mott review was scoped to 18 months from docketing.

Part 50 documentation centers on the PSAR (§ 50.34(a)) at the CP stage and the FSAR (§ 50.34(b)) at the OL stage, with format and content guidance in Regulatory Guide 1.70 Rev. 3 (1978, LWR Edition) and DANU-ISG-2022-05 for advanced reactors. An Environmental Report under 10 CFR §§ 51.49–51.50 supports NEPA review. An Updated FSAR (§ 50.71(e)) is maintained throughout operation, reflecting changes under § 50.59. Historical CP reviews took two to four years; OL reviews overlapped the last one to three years of construction. Watts Bar Unit 1 ran 23 years CP-to-OL (1973–1996); Unit 2 ran roughly 42 years owing to the 1985 construction suspension and 2007 reactivation.

Part 52 documentation includes an ESP application (Subpart A), a DC application that becomes an Appendix to Part 52 by rulemaking (Subpart B), and a COL application (Subpart C) that may incorporate the ESP and DC by reference. NRC's target schedule is 30 months for the combined safety and environmental review plus 12 months for the mandatory adjudicatory hearing — about 42 months application-to-COL. Vogtle 3 and 4 took about 47 months (March 2008 to February 2012). Construction-to-operation for Vogtle 3 ran about 10.5 years, driven by supply-chain, workforce, and Westinghouse-bankruptcy issues rather than Part 52 itself.

Part 53 documentation is structured around the Licensing-Basis Event analysis under § 53.240 and § 53.450, the site characterization under §§ 53.510 and 53.530, and the Subpart H licensing instrument the applicant chooses. Companion regulatory guidance — including a revision to RG 4.7 and guidance on codes, standards, maintenance, and inspection — remains in development, with the Staff Requirements Memorandum directing delivery by late 2027. First Part 53 applications are widely expected in 2026–2027, with NRC targeting reviews of 18 months or less.

sections.heading: How AI-assisted compliance changes the calculus
paragraphs: The shift toward 18-month review schedules, performance-based safety criteria, and Licensing-Basis Event analyses driven by PRA or Systematic Risk Evaluation places more weight on the precision and traceability of the documentation an applicant produces. A modern Part 50 PSAR for a non-LWR, a Part 52 COL FSAR referencing a Design Certification, and a Part 53 LBE analysis all share a common operational reality: thousands of cross-references between the safety case, the codes and standards invoked under § 50.55a, the quality-assurance program under Appendix B, the emergency plan under Appendix E, and the as-built configuration captured under § 50.71(e).

AI-assisted compliance tools — used as document-retrieval, cross-reference, and consistency-checking aids rather than as authors of safety conclusions — can reduce the labor cost of maintaining this web of references. In practice, that matters most where the regulatory framework itself is new or evolving: matching Part 53 LBE bins to historical Part 50 Chapter 15 analyses, mapping Principal Design Criteria for a non-LWR to the General Design Criteria expectations reviewers carry from decades of LWR practice, or tracing § 50.59 evaluations through an Updated FSAR. Used appropriately and with human review at every safety-significant step, these tools are a way to make the new 18-month schedules tractable without compromising the depth of the safety case.

faqs.question: What is the difference between 10 CFR Part 50, Part 52, and Part 53?
faqs.answer: Part 50 (1956) is a two-step licensing process: a Construction Permit issued against a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report under § 50.34(a), then a separate Operating License issued against a Final Safety Analysis Report under § 50.34(b). Part 52 (1989) is a one-step alternative: a Combined License that authorizes both construction and conditional operation, typically referencing an Early Site Permit and a Standard Design Certification, with ITAAC verified before the § 52.103(g) finding. Part 53 (effective April 29, 2026) is an optional risk-informed, technology-inclusive framework that replaces the LWR-centric General Design Criteria with performance-based safety criteria and a Licensing-Basis Event spectrum derived from PRA or Systematic Risk Evaluation.

faqs.question: Did Part 52 replace Part 50?
faqs.answer: No. Part 52 was issued April 18, 1989 (54 FR 15372) as an alternative to Part 50, not a replacement. Both pathways remain available, and applicants choose between them based on design maturity, fleet strategy, and tolerance for upfront regulatory burden. Essentially every operating U.S. commercial reactor was licensed under Part 50, and the pathway has been revived for first-of-a-kind non-LWR demonstrations including Kairos Hermes, TerraPower Natrium, X-energy/Dow Long Mott, and Holtec SMR-300 at Palisades.

faqs.question: Does Part 53 replace Part 50 or Part 52?
faqs.answer: No. Part 53 is explicitly optional. The final rule, published March 30, 2026 and effective April 29, 2026, adds a third pathway alongside Parts 50 and 52. Applicants can still choose Part 50 or Part 52, and within Part 53 itself Subpart H provides a menu of licensing instruments — construction permits, operating licenses, combined licenses, standard design approvals, design certifications, and manufacturing licenses.

faqs.question: Which reactors were licensed under Part 52?
faqs.answer: Vogtle Units 3 and 4 (Southern Nuclear, AP1000) are the only Part 52 plants in commercial operation; their Combined Licenses were issued February 10, 2012 and the § 52.103(g) findings came on August 3, 2022 (Unit 3) and July 28, 2023 (Unit 4). V.C. Summer Units 2 and 3 received COLs March 30, 2012 but construction was abandoned August 17, 2017. South Texas Project Units 3 and 4 received COLs February 12, 2016 but were never built. NuScale's Power Module received the first SMR Design Certification in 2023 (Appendix G to Part 52), and the uprated 77-MWe US460 received a Standard Design Approval May 29, 2025.

faqs.question: Why are advanced reactor developers choosing Part 50 over Part 52?
faqs.answer: Part 50's Construction Permit is issued against a Preliminary Safety Analysis Report that need only show 'reasonable assurance' the final design will protect public health and safety. Design information at the CP stage is preliminary and may change during construction, which suits first-of-a-kind non-LWR projects where iteration during fabrication and testing is expected. Part 52, by contrast, requires essentially Operating-License-level design detail at application time. Kairos Power, TerraPower, X-energy/Dow, and Holtec all chose Part 50 for their first commercial units for this reason, and the Vogtle 3 and 4 experience under Part 52 reinforced the preference.

faqs.question: What is ITAAC and how does it work under Part 52?
faqs.answer: Inspections, Tests, Analyses, and Acceptance Criteria (ITAAC) are the verification mechanism that makes a one-step Part 52 license safe. They are required in a Design Certification (§ 52.47) and a Combined License (§ 52.80), incorporated into the COL on issuance (§ 52.97), closed via ICN notifications (§ 52.99), and verified through the § 52.103(g) finding that the Commission must make before fuel load. The NRC publishes notice 180 days before scheduled fuel load, and any affected person has 60 days to request a pre-operational hearing on whether the as-built plant meets the acceptance criteria.

faqs.question: What is a Licensing-Basis Event under Part 53?
faqs.answer: Under § 53.240, Licensing-Basis Events (LBEs) are the full population of accident sequences identified using PRA or a Systematic Risk Evaluation. LBEs are binned by frequency: Anticipated Operational Occurrences (≥ 10⁻² /plant-yr), Design-Basis Events (10⁻² to 10⁻⁴ /plant-yr), and Beyond-Design-Basis Events (10⁻⁴ to ~5×10⁻⁷ /plant-yr). Each bin is analyzed under § 53.450 against the LMP frequency-consequence curve. This replaces the Part 50 deterministic 'single design-basis accident' approach with a quantitative consequence assessment across the entire LBE spectrum.

faqs.question: When does 10 CFR Part 53 take effect?
faqs.answer: The Commission voted to approve the final Part 53 rule on March 25–26, 2026; it was published in the Federal Register on March 30, 2026 (91 FR / Vol. 91, No. 60); and it took effect April 29, 2026. This was more than a year ahead of the December 31, 2027 deadline set by NEIMA Section 103(a)(4). Companion regulatory guidance — including a revision to RG 4.7 and guidance on codes, standards, maintenance, and inspection — remains in development through 2027.

faqs.question: How long does each licensing pathway take?
faqs.answer: Historical Part 50 reviews took two to four years for the CP and overlapped the last one to three years of construction for the OL; Watts Bar Unit 1 ran 23 years CP-to-OL. NRC's target schedule for a Part 52 COL referencing a Design Certification and an Early Site Permit is about 42 months (30 months for safety and environmental review plus 12 months for the mandatory hearing); Vogtle 3 and 4 took 47 months. Under NEIMA and Executive Order 14300 (2025), the NRC now targets 18 months for new reactor decisions; TerraPower's Kemmerer Unit 1 safety review finished in 18 months, nine months ahead of schedule.

faqs.question: What is a Standard Design Certification?
faqs.answer: A Standard Design Certification under Subpart B of Part 52 is a rulemaking — not a license — that certifies an 'essentially complete' standard reactor design. The certified design is codified as an Appendix to 10 CFR Part 52, giving it the force of regulation. Issues resolved in the rulemaking are subject to finality under § 52.63 and generally cannot be reopened in a downstream Combined License proceeding. Certified designs include the ABWR (Appendix A), AP1000 (Appendix D), ESBWR (Appendix E), APR1400 (Appendix F), and the NuScale Power Module US600 (Appendix G).

faqs.question: Can a developer switch pathways mid-application?
faqs.answer: In principle yes, but it requires re-docketing under the new framework and re-doing the safety case in the new format. In practice, developers commit to a pathway through their Regulatory Engagement Plan during pre-application engagement and stay with it. TerraPower locked in Part 50 for Kemmerer Unit 1 even though Part 53 became available before the CP was issued, because re-scoping mid-review would have eliminated the schedule advantage. Future units in a fleet may move to a different pathway.

faqs.question: What is the difference between Part 50 Appendix A (GDC) and Part 53 design criteria?
faqs.answer: Appendix A to Part 50 specifies 64 General Design Criteria organized into six groups — Overall Requirements, Protection by Multiple Fission Product Barriers, Protection and Reactivity Control Systems, Fluid Systems, Reactor Containment, and Fuel and Radioactivity Control — and the criteria embed LWR-centric assumptions. Non-LWR applicants under Parts 50 or 52 typically use Principal Design Criteria with guidance in Regulatory Guide 1.232. Part 53 replaces both with high-level performance criteria in §§ 53.210 and 53.220, evaluated against the LBE spectrum under §§ 53.240 and 53.450, with no LWR-specific prescriptive requirements at the top level.
